did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Graph Neural Networks (specifically Vision GNN or ViG architectures, including ViG-Ti and ViG-B variants) for skin lesion classification. The study implements these deep learning models on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset to classify skin lesions into seven categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma) and reports performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically implementing and evaluating Vision GNN (ViG-Ti and ViG-B) models on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing, model architecture, and training procedures, as well as a results section with performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) comparing the proposed models against baselines. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical findings and model performance, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Vision GNN: Advancing Skin Lesion Classification with Graph Neural Networks
journal: 2025 8th International Conference on Control, Robotics and Informatics (ICCRI)
brief_summary: This study evaluates Vision Graph Neural Networks (ViG) for dermatoscopic skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers implemented two ViG model variants, ViG-Ti and ViG-B, to address the challenges of irregular lesion morphology that traditional CNNs struggle with. To handle class imbalance, the training data was balanced via oversampling, and generalization was enhanced through various data augmentation techniques. The lightweight ViG-Ti model achieved state-of-the-art performance with 0.90 accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score, outperforming the larger ViG-B variant and established architectures like ResNet50 and EfficientNetB0. The results demonstrate that architectural efficiency and optimization can surpass model complexity for this specific medical imaging task. This approach offers a promising direction for efficient, accurate computer-aided dermatology diagnosis with lower computational requirements.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification; noted as the dominant class (~67% of training samples) before oversampling.

all_task_types.target: Seven dermatoscopic skin lesion categories (Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, part of the ISIC 2018 Challenge. It serves as a foundational benchmark for automated skin lesion analysis algorithms.

per_class.nv: Approximately 67% of training samples before oversampling
per_class.df: Less than 2% of training samples before oversampling
per_class.vasc: Less than 2% of training samples before oversampling

use.training: Used for training the Vision GNN (ViG-Ti and ViG-B) models. The training subset (8,012 images) was subjected to oversampling to balance class distribution and data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, perspective adjustment, brightness modification, color jitter, Gaussian noise) to enhance generalization.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. The test subset (2,003 images) was used to evaluate performance metrics including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using point estimates of accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Multiclass classification of seven skin lesion categories (nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df) after oversampling training data to balance class distribution
HAM10000.notes: ViG-Ti achieved state-of-the-art performance, outperforming ViG-B and all listed baselines. The model showed strong diagonal performance in the confusion matrix, particularly for 'nv' and 'bkl' classes, but exhibited moderate confusion between visually similar lesions (e.g., benign keratosis vs. melanoma). No statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) were reported.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (models were trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images underwent normalization, resizing, and enhancement)

architecture_details.core_mechanism: Graph-based representation where image patches are treated as nodes and connected to nearest neighbors
architecture_details.input_stage: Conv2D layer with 48 kernels, size 1x1, stride 1, padding 0
architecture_details.deep_gcn_stack: 11 repeated residual graph convolution blocks, each combining Grapher and FFN submodules
architecture_details.downsampling: Applied at 3 strategic locations to reduce spatial resolution and increase feature depth
architecture_details.output_stage: Produces global features serving as embeddings for the classification head

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Global features (embeddings) passed to a classification head for multiclass classification of 7 skin lesion categories

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a 30-degree angle as shown in Figure 5(b).

conclusions: The lightweight Vision GNN (ViG-Ti) model achieved state-of-the-art performance for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset, attaining 0.90 accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score.

The compact ViG-Ti architecture outperformed the larger ViG-B variant (0.87 across all metrics), demonstrating that architectural optimization and efficiency can surpass increased model complexity for dermatoscopic analysis.
The ViG-Ti model significantly outperformed established baseline architectures including ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, ShuffleNet, MobileNet, and YOLOv8x-cls.

Graph Neural Networks offer advantages over traditional CNNs for skin lesion classification by using flexible graph-based representations that better accommodate the irregular, non-rectangular morphology of skin lesions compared to rigid grid structures.
Class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset was effectively addressed through oversampling, which balanced class distribution and improved model generalization across all lesion categories.

Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, perspective adjustment, brightness modification, color jitter, and Gaussian noise) were effective in preventing overfitting and enhancing generalization.

While the model showed strong performance on dominant classes like Melanocytic Nevi (nv) and Benign Keratosis (bkl), it exhibited moderate confusion between visually similar lesion types, particularly among melanocytic lesions.

The study establishes that a carefully optimized lightweight architecture can provide efficient, accurate computer-aided dermatology diagnosis with lower computational requirements compared to heavier models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a multi-source collection; performance may not generalize to other populations or clinical settings without external validation.

Class imbalance was addressed via oversampling of the training set, which may lead to data leakage if test images are duplicates of oversampled training images, though the paper does not explicitly confirm the exclusion of duplicates from the test split.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of ViG-Ti over baselines; the authors note this as a limitation and suggest McNemar's test for future work.
The model exhibits moderate confusion between visually similar pathologies, specifically benign keratosis (bkl) and melanoma (mel), indicating potential diagnostic risks in clinical deployment.

Performance on minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions) remains limited due to data scarcity, even after oversampling, as these classes constituted less than 2% of the original training data.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) for individual lesion types, only aggregate macro/micro averages, making it difficult to assess sensitivity for critical classes like melanoma specifically.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope; it does not address deployment concerns such as inference speed, hardware requirements beyond training, or integration into clinical workflows.
